post_mortem: The thesis leaned hard on Burrows' ugly 5.77 season ERA, but missed a critical split: Burrows carried a 3.12 ERA with three quality starts in his last four road outings — this was a road start, and he delivered again, holding KC to 7 runs across 24 batters on 92 pitches. Meanwhile, Cameron was pulled early (only 20 batters faced, 86 pitches) and the Astros' power — Walker, Meyers, and Matthews all homered — did the damage. The model also ignored KC's 13-of-last-18 home losing streak, which undercut the home-field advantage assumption entirely.
candidate_rule: Before fading a starter on ERA alone, check road/home splits for the last 4-5 starts — if the road ERA diverges from season ERA by 2+ runs, the season number is not the operative number for this game; N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — but the split-blindness pattern is worth flagging as a recurring model vulnerability to monitor.

post_mortem: The under was torched by a mid-game injury that nobody could model. Strider's velocity cratered inning-by-inning — from 95.7 mph in the 1st to 88.7 mph in the 4th — and before he exited with right arm soreness, he'd already surrendered a season-high 7 runs on 6 hits in 3-plus innings, including back-to-back solo HRs to Bichette and Soto in the 1st and a Bichette grand slam in the 2nd. The game was 7-2 before the 5th inning even started; the under was dead on arrival the moment Strider's elbow gave out, not because the thesis was wrong.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. The injury was unforeseeable mid-game; Strider entered the start healthy and had posted a 4.00 ERA across 7 prior outings. No pre-game signal flagged this.

post_mortem: The thesis leaned entirely on Sasaki replicating his Angels masterpiece, but he reverted to his early-season self: 91 pitches, only 53 strikes, and a catastrophic 5th inning in which Chicago sent 11 batters to the plate and scored 7 runs — including a tiebreaking Vargas RBI double and a bases-loaded walk on 4 pitches to Braden Montgomery. Sasaki finished 4.1 IP, 7 R, 7 H, 3 BB, and was pulled before recording the 5th-inning out. A compounding DATA wrinkle: Shohei Ohtani was scratched pre-game with knee inflammation, quietly gutting the Dodgers' most dangerous bat from the lineup the model assumed would be active.
candidate_rule: Before capping an Under on a Sasaki start, require his last-7-days command metrics (BB/9, Strike%) to confirm the Angels outing wasn't an outlier — one elite start does not establish a trend; N=1 on the Ohtani-scratch-deflates-Dodgers-offense pattern, also insufficient to formalize.
sample_size_warning: N=1 loss, insufficient to establish a rule. Single-start regression is inherent pitcher variance, but the structural error — projecting forward from one career-best outing without confirming command stability — is a repeatable model mistake worth tracking.

post_mortem: The thesis played out almost perfectly through eight innings — Singer bounced back with 6 IP / 2 ER, King was solid, and the Reds' bats (Bleday 391-ft solo HR in the 7th, Suárez 365-ft HR in the 8th) gave Cincinnati a 4-2 lead heading into the bottom of the 8th. The Padres run line was alive and the pick was winning. Then the Reds bullpen imploded: Caleb Ferguson couldn't retire the lefties he faced in the 8th, Tony Santillan couldn't bail him out, and a Gavin Sheets RBI double tied it at 4-4. In the bottom of the 9th, Chase Petty — a Triple-A callup brought in specifically for bullpen depth — grooved an 89 mph slider to Fernando Tatis Jr., who lined a walk-off homer just over the left-field wall to end it 5-4. The Padres won the game; they just didn't win by 2.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — this is a clean bullpen-blowup variance loss, not a model flaw.

post_mortem: The entire under thesis rested on Simeon Woods Richardson (7.74 ERA) starting for Toronto — but the Blue Jays actually sent Mason Fluharty (3-0, 3.97 ERA, 1.37 WHIP), a legitimate MLB arm, to the mound. The pick was made before Toronto officially announced their starter, and the 'replacement-level arm' data point that justified fading Toronto's run contribution never materialized. With Fluharty holding and Toronto winning 7-2, the over hit by nearly three runs — not because Sale imploded, but because the opposing pitcher mismatch the model priced in simply didn't exist on game day.
candidate_rule: Do not finalize a total bet when the opposing starter is unannounced or listed as TBD; require official lineup confirmation before locking the pick, especially when the starter identity is the primary driver of the thesis — N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — but the failure mode (betting a total against an unconfirmed starter) is structurally repeatable and worth flagging as a process check.

post_mortem: The wind data was wrong in the most damaging way possible: pre-game forecasts cited 13 mph blowing IN from left-center, but multiple pre-game sources noted the wind was actually blowing OUT — PrizePicks flagged 'wind blowing straight out at around 11 mph' giving a 34% HR boost per Ballpark Pal, and Bleed Cubbie Blue noted pregame 'the wind will be howling out to left.' The entire thesis rested on wind suppressing Imanaga's fly-ball/HR problem; instead, the park played as a hitter's environment. The result was four home runs on the night — back-to-back shots from Soderstrom and Heim off Imanaga in the 7th to make it 6-1, plus Langeliers' solo HR in the 4th and a Crow-Armstrong HR off Ginn — and the Cubs' 9th-inning walk-off rally (Happ RBI double, Ballesteros infield single, Swanson game-tying single, PCA walk-off RBI single) pushed the final to 7-6, one run over the 10.5 total.
candidate_rule: Before posting any Wrigley under that leans on wind-in suppression, cross-check at least two real-time wind sources (e.g., Ballpark Pal + Weather.com game-time reading) within 2 hours of first pitch, and void the pick if any source shows wind blowing out — N=1, insufficient to establish rule, but the directional error here was the entire load-bearing assumption.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. One wind-data miss does not confirm a systematic sourcing failure, but the magnitude of the directional error (in vs. out) warrants an immediate pre-game wind verification step in the checklist.

post_mortem: Wembanyama finished with exactly 26 points — one short of the 26.5 line — but the story isn't bad luck at the margin. He shot 6-of-21 from the field (2-of-9 from three) with six turnovers, and the Knicks successfully kept him away from the rim: only 5 of his 21 attempts came at the basket, forcing him into the perimeter jumpers the thesis assumed would be available in the paint. The model leaned on his WCF PPG average (27.3) while underweighting his full 17-game playoff PPG (23.2) and ignored the specific defensive wrinkle — the Knicks didn't leave the paint open as predicted; they funneled him to the perimeter and let him shoot over traffic. He self-described the performance as 'bad,' and the 12-of-13 free throw line production (the only thing that got him to 26) masked a genuinely poor shooting night.
candidate_rule: When a player's series-specific sample (WCF, N=7) diverges materially from their full playoff sample (N=17), weight the larger sample more heavily in the points projection — especially when the opponent's defensive scheme is structurally different from the prior series opponent; validate this rule at N≥10 before applying as a hard filter.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — this is a single-game observation and could reflect Finals debut variance as much as a systematic modeling error.

post_mortem: Final was 105-95 (200 combined), missing the Over 217.5 by 17.5. Both primary scorers were ice-cold — Wembanyama shot 3-of-13 and Brunson shot 7-of-22 through three quarters — and the Spurs bench outside of Dylan Harper combined for just 4 points. The thesis extrapolated Spurs WCF home-game pace (226.4 PPG) directly onto a Finals Game 1, ignoring that opponent quality, defensive intensity, and game-script all reset in a new series. The 'case against' even flagged a potential defensive feel-out game, but the model still leaned into the over.
candidate_rule: Do not use a team's same-round home-game scoring averages as a baseline for a new series — opponent defensive quality resets; require at least one data point from the new matchup or a head-to-head regular season pace proxy before projecting totals above the market line. (N=1, insufficient to establish rule.)
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — one game where both stars shot poorly and a bench went cold is noise, not a pattern.

post_mortem: The thesis was built entirely on Harrison suppressing the Giants — and he did exactly that, striking out 12 over 5.2 innings and allowing just 1 run. The model failure was on the other half of the equation: McDonald. The pick treated McDonald's 4.34 ERA as a manageable risk, but the Brewers jumped on him immediately — Jake Bauers hit a 3-run homer in the first inning after Yelich and Chourio walked, and Milwaukee built a 4-0 lead before the Giants could respond. The Giants briefly made it 4-3 in the eighth on pinch-hit RBI singles, but the Brewers then blew it open with a 4-run bottom of the eighth against Tristan Beck — Yelich, Chourio, and Turang all drove in runs — pushing the final to 8-3. The under was never really alive: Milwaukee's home run-scoring rate (5.42 R/G at home) and McDonald's contact-heavy profile were a combustible combination that the model underweighted in favor of fixating on Harrison's floor.
candidate_rule: When the under thesis depends on a single elite starter suppressing one side, require the opposing starter's FIP to be within 1.0 of the total-implied run expectation per team — if the gap is wider, the under is structurally one-sided and the total is more likely to be driven by the weaker arm; flag as MODEL RISK before publishing. (N=1, insufficient to establish rule — monitor for pattern across similar 'ace carries the under' setups.)
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule.

post_mortem: The thesis was built on Martin's season-long dominance, but two critical factors were underweighted: (1) Martin's career ERA against Minnesota entering this start was 5.29 in seven games — a specific, documented vulnerability that the pick glossed over in favor of his one dominant outing five days prior; (2) the 'short turnaround repeat matchup' risk cut both ways — Prielipp, who got shelled in that prior start, went a career-high six innings and struck out seven, while the Twins' bottom-of-the-order hitters torched Martin for a season-high 10 hits and 6 runs in 4⅔ innings, blowing up the 'floor of 6 innings of 2-run ball' assumption entirely. The White Sox took a 3-0 lead in the third on Acuña's aggressive baserunning and a Vargas two-run single, but Minnesota answered with a four-run fourth inning — Martin walked two batters in the fifth and allowed another run before being pulled. The Twins bullpen then struck out the side in the seventh with the tying run at second, slamming the door. The pick leaned on recency bias (one great start vs. MIN) while ignoring Martin's documented career struggles against this specific opponent.
candidate_rule: Before backing a starter as a repeat-matchup favorite within 7 days, cross-check career splits against that opponent — if career ERA vs. that team is 1.5+ runs above season ERA, flag as a MODEL risk and require a line of -110 or better to proceed; N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — this is a pattern to watch, not a validated filter.

post_mortem: The thesis held for exactly 12 minutes. Carolina jumped out 2-0 on Ehlers goals at 0:25 and 12:08 of the first — the pick was alive and the defensive structure looked intact. Then Vegas did something road teams had never done in Cup Final Game 1 history: they erased a multi-goal deficit (0-55 all-time entering tonight) and won 5-4. The game was tied 4-4 with 8:41 left in the third after Gostisbehere's tying goal, meaning Carolina had a legitimate chance to win regulation. The killing blow was Hertl's slot goal at 16:36 of the third off a Sissons feed — a single shot in a game that was dead-even for 55+ minutes. Andersen allowed 5 goals on just 23 shots, a .783 SV% that was a complete outlier against his .944 playoff mark; Carolina's top line of Aho/Jarvis/Svechnikov went pointless and was outscored 1-0 at 5v5. This was not a bad pick — it was a correctly reasoned pick that ran into a historically improbable Vegas comeback, a cold night from the Conn Smythe frontrunner, and one late goal that could have gone either way.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. Carolina's defensive structure and Andersen's save percentage remain elite indicators — one outlier game does not invalidate the thesis.

pick: Bobby Witt Jr. Total Bases Over 1.5
post_mortem: Abbott was not the command-challenged lefty the thesis described — he had posted a 1.85 ERA over his last six starts entering this game, with tightened control, and he rolled through KC's lineup in a 10-inning, 4-3 Reds win. Witt went 0-for on the night and was stranded twice with runners in scoring position and 2 outs, suggesting weak contact or punchouts rather than hard-luck outs. The pick leaned on Abbott's season-aggregate HR/9 (1.30) and BB% (10%) without adequately discounting his recent hot stretch — a classic recency-blind model error.
candidate_rule: When a starting pitcher's ERA over his last 6 starts is more than 2 runs below his season ERA, flag the pick and re-evaluate using recent-form metrics rather than season aggregates before publishing — but apply only after N≥10 similar cases to confirm the pattern holds.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — this is a directional flag only.

post_mortem: The thesis was inverted: the pick targeted Marte feasting on a hittable Lauer, but Marte bats for Arizona — he faced Michael Soroka (7-2, 3.25 ERA), not Lauer. Soroka faced 25 batters and held Arizona's lineup in check; the CBS box score shows Marte's only recorded action against Lauer was a groundout to the pitcher, and his lone RBI contribution was a sacrifice fly (0 TB). The model correctly identified Lauer as hittable but failed to account for which side of the ball Marte plays on — the Dodgers' lineup faced Lauer, not the Diamondbacks'.
candidate_rule: Before submitting any player prop, validate that the targeted hitter faces the identified weak pitcher — not the opposing starter; a directional mismatch (hitter vs. wrong pitcher) is a disqualifying error that should void the pick pre-submission. (N=1, insufficient to establish rule.)
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — but this is a process/logic error, not a sample size question. The mismatch should be caught at pick-generation time regardless of sample.

post_mortem: The thesis was built on deGrom's May struggles (5.72 ERA), but the pick ignored a critical counter-signal hiding in plain sight: deGrom entered this start with a 3.77 season ERA, a 10.56 K/9, and a .205 opponent batting average over his last 10 outings — he was trending sharply in the right direction, not still mired in May. On the night, he was vintage deGrom: five shutout innings, 4 hits, 1 walk, 8 strikeouts, earning his 100th career win. The Cardinals had runners on in the 2nd inning but deGrom struck out Walker and Torres to escape, then held them scoreless through 5. McGreevy, meanwhile, gave up the two decisive runs in the 4th (Nimmo scored on a Duran double) and 5th (Jansen walked, stole second, scored on a Pederson single), and the Cardinals' lone run — a Masyn Winn solo homer off reliever Peyton Gray in the 6th — was too little, too late. The bullpen held the 2-1 lead and Latz closed it in the 9th. The model leaned on a stale May ERA snapshot while deGrom's recent form and elite strikeout profile — the exact 'case against' acknowledged in the pick — was the actual signal.
candidate_rule: When a starter's trailing-month ERA diverges significantly from their season ERA AND their last-10-start ERA is trending down, weight the recent trend over the monthly split before fading them — but validate this rule across a minimum of 20+ comparable 'ERA-rebound' starts before treating it as a systematic edge.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule; this is a single-game observation and could easily be noise.

post_mortem: The thesis was sound — Judge faced a struggling Lopez (5.73 ERA, 2.05 HR/9) and the Yankees won the game. But Lopez and the A's bullpen pitched around Judge relentlessly: the play-by-play shows Judge drew multiple walks off Lopez and was intentionally avoided in key spots, producing only 1 total base (an RBI single per the ESPN box score showing 'Judge 1-2' with RISP). The cruelest moment came in the 5th inning of the May 30 game (wrong game — confirmed May 31 data shows Judge went 1-for-2 with a walk and an RBI, totaling exactly 1 base), where a deep fly to right off Ginn was caught at the wall — described as 'a home run in 16 ballparks, including Yankee Stadium.' Judge hit the ball hard, got on base multiple times, and drove in a run, but the A's strategy of issuing walks and one loud out at the warning track held him to exactly the 1.5 line threshold.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — this is a single-game prop outcome driven by opponent walk strategy and one warning-track out, not a systematic model failure.

post_mortem: The game was actually alive for the under through six innings — Bibee held a 4-3 lead and was on track to exit with the score well within range. But the thesis had a critical blind spot: Bibee entered this start having surrendered a franchise record-tying five home runs in three innings against Washington his previous outing, and the Boston Globe's pre-game notes flagged that Suarez had a bimodal pattern — either dominant or shelled, with four-plus runs allowed in four of his ten starts. The under died in the seventh when Boston sent 10 batters to the plate against three Cleveland relievers, scoring six runs to turn a 4-3 deficit into a 9-4 final. The 'both bullpens are solid' assumption was the kill shot — Cleveland's relief corps collapsed at the exact moment the game needed them most.
candidate_rule: Before backing an under involving Bibee, require his last-start HR/9 and hard-hit rate to be within 1.0 of his season average — a single blowup start is a warning sign of mechanical issues that carry over; N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — monitor Bibee under picks for pattern over next 4-6 starts.

post_mortem: The thesis was sound — Messick (2.24 ERA, 1.04 WHIP) vs. a Boston offense ranked 29th in runs scored looked like a textbook under setup. But Messick got lit up in what became his worst start of the season, while Sonny Gray — the 'solid but short' arm we were worried about — turned in six innings of one-run ball. Per ESPN, 'Connor Wong and Jarren Duran drove in three runs apiece' as Boston rolled to a 9-1 final. The box score shows Messick was pulled early (only 93 pitches logged before the bullpen took over), and the Cleveland pen surrendered a cascade of runs via a Ramirez error, a Fairchild throwing error, a Rocchio error, and a Jarren Duran three-run homer that blew the game open. The under was dead by the middle innings — this wasn't a late-game collapse, it was Messick's outlier bad start (his 12th of the season, against a team he'd only faced once before and allowed 9 hits in 3.2 IP in that prior outing) combined with a multi-error defensive meltdown behind him.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. Messick's one prior career start vs. BOS (3 runs, 9 hits in 3.2 IP) was a known data point in the case-against but was underweighted — worth monitoring whether his splits vs. AL East contact-heavy lineups diverge from his overall numbers, but a single-game blowout is noise.

post_mortem: The thesis leaned on Meyer's ERA and Peralta's K-rate while ignoring Peralta's known Marlins-specific vulnerability (career 4.18 ERA vs. MIA) and his 8 HR allowed in 61.1 IP — Liam Hicks had 2 HR in 6 prior BvP PA against him and delivered again. Meyer himself got torched in a 4-run Mets inning (Bichette single → Soto walk → Young walk → Ewing 2-RBI single → Baty 2-RBI single), and a depleted Mets bullpen (missing Senga, Burdi, Herget, and others) had no margin to hold the game once Peralta exited. The final 9-7 (16 total) wasn't a fluke — the structural conditions for a high-scoring game were present in the pre-game data and were underweighted.
candidate_rule: When a starter has a HR/9 above 1.10 AND a documented negative BvP split against the opposing lineup's cleanup hitter (2+ HR in ≤8 PA), flag the Under as degraded regardless of ERA headline — but validate against a larger sample (N=1 here, insufficient to establish rule).
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. Pattern to watch: Under picks where one starter has a sub-1.30 ERA but a 1.10+ HR/9 rate and a depleted home bullpen behind them.

post_mortem: The thesis leaned entirely on Skenes suppressing the Cubs, but the pick ignored two compounding red flags that were publicly available pre-game: Skenes had allowed 9 earned runs across his prior two starts (Phillies and Blue Jays), was entering what would become his first-ever three-game losing streak, and his pitch count was already at 70 through just four innings on Thursday. The game unraveled in the 5th-6th: Skenes walked Busch, surrendered an infield single and a bloop single to Suzuki that scored the first run, then a Pirates defensive error on an infield grounder by Happ let another run score before Skenes was pulled at 5.1 innings — well short of the '6+ innings' the thesis required. The Cubs bullpen then teed off on a depleted Pirates relief corps (four relievers used the night before), with Ian Happ's two-run homer in the 8th pushing it to 5-2, and Chicago added two more to finish 7-2. The under was never really alive: the 'case against' section of the pick itself flagged Skenes' shakiness and bullpen exposure, but the model still rated confidence at 3 and played the unit — the data didn't support the conviction.
candidate_rule: When the named anchor starter has allowed 3+ ER in each of his prior two starts AND is projected for a rising pitch count (70+ through 4 IP in recent outings), discount under confidence by at least one tier and flag bullpen exposure as a primary risk, not a footnote — but validate across a minimum of 15–20 similar starter-fatigue under spots before codifying as a hard filter.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule; this is a directional signal only and requires pattern confirmation across a larger sample of 'ace in mini-slump' under bets.

post_mortem: The entire thesis rested on Elder dominating his half — he didn't come close. Elder was knocked out in 3⅓ innings, surrendering 6 runs on 9 hits (including 2 errors that extended the damage) in a catastrophic 4th inning where BOS sent 9+ batters to the plate and scored 6 runs via singles, errors, and a wild pitch. The under was dead by the 4th inning; this wasn't a late-game variance event. The model over-indexed on Elder's season-long ERA (1.97) without accounting for the risk of a short, blow-up start — a single bad outing from the anchor pitcher destroys the under regardless of how good the opposing pitcher is.
candidate_rule: When the under thesis is structurally dependent on one pitcher going deep (6+ IP, ≤2 ER), apply a 'short-start discount' — if that pitcher's start-length variance (e.g. % of starts under 5 IP) exceeds 15%, reduce confidence by at least 1 unit; N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — single data point, monitoring for pattern of over-reliance on ERA without start-length floor validation.

post_mortem: The thesis leaned on home-ice momentum and a must-win atmosphere, but Carolina dismantled that narrative in the first period alone — Aho (PP goal at 14:58), Staal (deflection at 16:07), and Stankoven (2-on-1 rush at 17:46) scored three times in a 2:47 span to make it 3-0 before the period was over, and the Bell Centre crowd was dead. Frederik Andersen needed only 18 saves for his third postseason shutout, while Dobes faced 39 shots — the Canadiens were outshot 19-3 in the third period alone, with the crowd audibly chanting 'Shoot the puck!' at their own team. The core model error was treating Game 1's 6-2 blowout as signal that Montreal could 'compete' with Carolina, when in reality Carolina had entered that game flat after an 11-day layoff off a sweep; Games 2-4 showed the true talent gap once the Hurricanes were locked in.
candidate_rule: When a team's best win in a series came against a rested opponent returning from a long layoff (11+ days), discount that result as a talent signal and weight the subsequent games more heavily before pricing home-dog value — but N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule.

post_mortem: The entire thesis rested on two starters — Ashcraft held up his end completely (1 R, 6.1 IP, quality start), but the Cubs' half of the equation was a ghost. Jordan Wicks was not a real MLB starter: he had a career 5.21 ERA, had spent 2025 exclusively as a reliever with a 6.28 ERA in 14.1 innings, and was recalled from Triple-A Iowa on Sunday — the day before the game — only because Edward Cabrera went on the IL with a finger blister. The pack flagged 'thin data' on Wicks but still priced the under as if a competent starter was taking the mound. He was rocked for 8 runs on 9 hits in 4.1 innings: a Reynolds two-run double and a Valdez 415-foot two-run homer made it 5-0 in the first inning alone, and the game was effectively over before Ashcraft threw his second pitch. Final: PIT 12, CHC 1 — 11 of Pittsburgh's 13 runs came off Wicks and the Cubs' mop-up arms, not Ashcraft.
candidate_rule: When the Cubs' (or any team's) listed starter is a same-day recall from Triple-A with a career MLB ERA above 5.00 and no MLB starts in the current season, treat the opposing half of the total as uncapped and do not play unders unless the total is set 1.5+ runs above the ace's expected contribution alone — N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. One emergency-callup implosion is noise, but the DATA failure here (no MLB 2026 stats existed for Wicks at pick time, and the pack explicitly flagged it) is a process gap worth tracking.

post_mortem: The entire thesis rested on Suarez's elite HR suppression anchoring the under, but the kill shot came from the wrong side of the matchup: Suarez himself collapsed. He sailed through four scoreless innings, then surrendered two runs in the fifth on Matt Olson's 15th homer (a two-run shot that tied it 2-2), and was chased without recording an out in the sixth after Austin Riley tripled, Eli White walked, and Michael Harris II delivered an RBI double — Suarez was charged with five earned runs on six hits over five-plus innings, walking three and hitting a batter, the worst outing of his season. Atlanta's three-run sixth made it 5-2, Harris added a two-run homer in the eighth off the Boston bullpen, and despite three Boston home runs (Duran, Rafaela, Kiner-Falefa), the Red Sox fell 7-6 — 13 combined runs, blowing past the 8.0 total. The model correctly flagged Strider's walk rate as a risk but misidentified which pitcher would blow up: Strider allowed only 3 runs in 5 innings and settled in after the first, while Suarez — the supposed anchor — had his worst start of the year. The thesis was built on the wrong pitcher's floor.
candidate_rule: When anchoring an under on a specific starter's ERA/HR suppression, cross-check whether that pitcher has a recent 'cluster' of bad starts masked by a low season ERA — Suarez had allowed 12 of his 13 earned runs in just 13 innings across a subset of starts, a red flag that the ERA was misleading about his true variance profile; flag starters whose ERA and xERA diverge by >0.75 runs for under consideration (N=1, insufficient to establish rule).
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — one Suarez blowup does not confirm a systematic ERA-masking pattern, but the pre-game data point (12 of 13 ER in 13 innings of bad starts) was available and should have widened the confidence interval on his floor.

post_mortem: Ben Brown delivered exactly the gem the thesis demanded — he was dominant through his outing, with Brandon Lowe's RBI double in the 3rd being the only real damage Brown allowed. The Cubs tied it 1-1 on a Michael Busch solo HR in the 5th, and the game was still very much alive. The loss turned on a single at-bat in the 7th: reliever Trent Thornton surrendered a solo home run to light-hitting catcher Henry Davis, a low-leverage, low-probability outcome that handed Pittsburgh a 2-1 lead the Cubs never recovered from. The Cubs offense went 6-for-the-game with just one extra-base hit and stranded five runners in the first three innings alone — but the decisive run was a bullpen blowup against a bottom-of-the-order bat, not a starter failure or a lineup collapse.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. The thesis (starter edge + lineup depth) was sound and nearly correct — this is a one-game bullpen variance event, not a signal.

post_mortem: The entire thesis rested on Yesavage being Yesavage — but this was the start where the small sample finally regressed. He allowed a career-high five runs on five hits across 6.2 innings, surrendering more earned runs in one outing than he had in his previous five starts combined. Meanwhile, Junk — the supposed piñata — held Toronto to one run on eight singles across five innings, walking nobody and winning for the first time since April 28. The model correctly identified the pitching gap but built a run-line bet on a 25-inning ERA that was always one bad start away from evaporating; the 'case against' section even flagged this exact scenario and was overruled. Compounding matters, Vladimir Guerrero Jr. sat out with an elbow injury, quietly removing Toronto's most dangerous bat from the lineup the model priced in.
candidate_rule: Do not back a run-line favorite when the anchor starter has fewer than 40 IP in the current season AND the implied win margin requires both the starter to hold AND the opposing back-end arm to implode — two independent events that must co-occur; N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — but the failure mode (tiny-sample ERA regression + key lineup scratch) is a known structural risk that the pick's own 'case against' section identified and dismissed.

post_mortem: The entire thesis was directionally correct — Imai walked three of the first four batters he faced and loaded the bases in the first inning, exactly the meltdown scenario we modeled. But he escaped via a double play, then retired 16 of the final 17 Rangers he faced en route to six no-hit innings on 97 pitches. Relievers Steven Okert and debutant Alimber Santa finished the combined no-hitter — the first in MLB since 2024 — in a 9-0 Houston win. Rocker, the 'competent league-average arm' we backed, allowed four runs in five innings and took the loss. The pick lost not because the Imai walk-rate thesis was wrong (he walked four on the night), but because a historically rare event — a combined no-hitter thrown by a pitcher entering with an 8.31 ERA — intervened. Texas was no-hit while only striking out three times; they simply couldn't find a hit despite making contact all night.
sample_size_warning: N=1, pure VARIANCE — a combined no-hitter is a ~0.3% per-game event; no rule can be derived from this outcome.

post_mortem: The result_score field ('CLE 0 · PHI 3') is incorrect — that was Saturday's Zack Wheeler shutout (May 23). The actual pick game on May 24 ended Guardians 3, Phillies 1: Cleveland won. Messick threw 5.2 shutout innings (5 H, 2 BB, 6 K), Painter allowed 2 runs in 6.1 innings, and Travis Bazzana hit an insurance solo HR in the 8th. The thesis played out almost exactly as written — Messick dominated PHI's right-handed hitters (1-for-13, 5 K), Painter was hittable, and Cleveland took the series. This is a data entry error, not a loss.
candidate_rule: Before running autopsy, cross-validate result_score against an external box score source — a misattributed score from an adjacent game in the same series is a known failure mode; add a date-stamp check against the game_id or box score URL (N=1 occurrence, insufficient to establish pattern).
sample_size_warning: N=1 data-entry error observed; watching for pattern of same-series score confusion before formalizing a validation rule.

post_mortem: New York led wire-to-wire — the 'home elimination urgency' thesis never materialized. The Knicks jumped out 9-1 inside two minutes, led 37-27 after Q1, 60-54 at half, and 91-82 after three quarters. The kill shot came in Q4 when Landry Shamet hit three 3-pointers in a 99-second span to push it to 105-94, putting the game out of reach. Mitchell finished with only 23 points and Cleveland shot a brutal 12-of-41 from three — but the Cavs were never competitive enough for the spread to even be in play. The model ignored a critical structural factor: Cleveland was already showing signs of fatigue from a seven-game series against Toronto/Detroit, the Knicks were riding a 9-game playoff win streak with a plus-221 point differential (an NBA record through 12 games), and New York was 7-1 all-time in playoff games at Cleveland. The 'home elimination urgency' edge was real in theory but was overwhelmed by a Knicks team operating at a historically dominant level.
candidate_rule: Before backing a home favorite in a must-win elimination game, validate opponent's current playoff momentum streak and series point differential — if the road team's differential exceeds +150 over 10+ games, fade the home urgency narrative regardless of spread value; N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — one game where a dominant road team steamrolled a fatigued home side does not confirm the pattern.

post_mortem: The under died on Janson Junk, not Chris Sale. Sale was excellent — 7 IP, 1 ER, 8 K — and held up his end of the thesis perfectly. But Junk was a fire-starter in disguise: he entered with a 6.35 ERA in May and had surrendered 7 runs over 5.2 innings to the Rays just five days earlier. The Braves tagged him for a 3-run Riley homer in the 2nd and a 3-run Smith homer in the 6th, putting up 8 runs off Junk alone. The model correctly identified Sale's side of the ledger but materially underweighted Junk's recent collapse — his 4.14 ERA was a season-long average masking a brutal May, and the 'case against' section even flagged the Braves jumping on Junk early as the primary risk, then didn't adjust the confidence or units accordingly.
candidate_rule: When the opposing starter's current-month ERA diverges from his season ERA by 2+ runs (Junk: 4.14 season / 6.35 May), treat the under as structurally compromised and require the total to be set at least 0.5 below the implied run total before playing it — N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. One loss on a bad-month ERA divergence is noise; track over minimum 10 comparable starts before codifying.

post_mortem: The Mets built a 5-0 lead and the thesis was playing out — then a once-in-a-generation play erased it. With the bases loaded in the 2nd, McLean's sweeper to James Wood glanced off debutant Nick Morabito's arm in left field and caromed into center, allowing Wood to score an inside-the-park grand slam — only the 9th such play since 1994. The Nationals then added three more in the 3rd on a homer, a passed ball, and a sac fly to go up 7-5. McLean's final line (9 R, 6 ER, 5.2 IP) was a career-worst, but three of those runs were unearned and the decisive blow was a freak defensive misplay on a ball that had no business leaving the infield. The pick was correctly reasoned; a ball bouncing off a rookie's arm in the 2nd inning is not a model failure.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule; the inside-the-park grand slam is a ~0.1% frequency event and not a repeatable risk factor to model against.

post_mortem: The thesis correctly identified Rogers as a liability but fatally misread HOW he damages you. The pick's own 'case against' warned Rogers hurts via solo shots and walks — not clusters of singles — yet Rogers sent 10 batters to the plate in a five-run second inning, exactly the station-to-station cluster damage the model said was unlikely. Tampa Bay then batted around again in a four-run sixth, capped by Caminero's three-run homer, pushing the total to 22 runs (TB 16, BAL 6) — nearly triple the 7.5 line. McClanahan did his job (held Baltimore scoreless into the third, extending his streak to 23⅔ innings), but the under was already dead by the end of the second inning on Rogers alone. The model's core error: it discounted the 'big inning via traffic' scenario for Rogers despite his 1.54 WHIP screaming exactly that risk.
candidate_rule: When a starter carries a WHIP above 1.45, do not lean Under regardless of the opposing ace — high-WHIP pitchers generate multi-run innings via baserunner accumulation at a rate that overwhelms even elite run suppression on the other side; flag any Under pick where one starter's WHIP exceeds 1.40 for manual review. (N=1, insufficient to establish rule — monitor for pattern.)
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. One blowup from a high-WHIP starter does not validate a blanket filter — watch for recurrence across 10+ similar setups before codifying.

post_mortem: This game was a double-overtime Western Conference Finals opener — OKC was not blown out. Per CBS Sports, the Spurs were up 10 with 9:10 left in regulation, OKC clawed back, and the lead changed hands twice with the game tied three times in under two minutes before the clock ran out on regulation. Wembanyama (41 pts, 24 reb) and Harper (24 pts, 7 steals) took over in double OT to seal it — the Thunder -6.5 cover was alive deep into the fourth quarter and was ultimately killed by two overtime periods, not by a bad thesis.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. The pick was correctly reasoned — OKC led or was competitive through regulation — and was undone by a specific, low-probability double-OT outcome, not a structural model failure.

post_mortem: The thesis was predicated on Giolito being the weak link — he wasn't. Giolito allowed one hit through five innings in a strong debut. The loss came from Kirby, who was torched for five runs in the sixth: Sheets hit two homers off him (including a hanging changeup in the 6th), Merrill added an RBI double, and France's two-out single made it 7-0. Kirby entered the game 0-2 with a 10.38 ERA in two prior starts vs. the Padres — a red flag the pick acknowledged but underweighted, calling it a 1-unit play instead of a pass.
candidate_rule: When the starting pitcher on the 'safe' side of an under has a documented ERA above 8.00 in multiple prior starts against the opposing lineup, treat that as a disqualifying factor for the under regardless of park factors — but validate against a larger sample before hardcoding.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. One loss where Kirby's Padres-specific splits were the deciding factor is noise; need N≥5 similar setups to confirm the pattern.

post_mortem: Through nine innings this game was exactly what the thesis promised: a 2-2 tie, both starters doing their jobs (Martinez held Miami to 1 run through 7, Alcantara surrendered 7 hits in 6 innings but kept it close), and the under alive with room to spare. Then the 10th inning arrived and the Rays' bullpen — specifically Hunter Bigge — imploded catastrophically, surrendering 8 runs on 6 hits including a Liam Hicks two-run single and a Javier Sanoja double that plated three, turning a 2-2 game into a 10-2 final before Tampa added 3 garbage runs of their own. The under was breathing comfortably at 4 total runs through 9; a single bullpen meltdown in extras buried it.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — this is a clean VARIANCE loss, not a modeling failure requiring a corrective rule.

post_mortem: The thesis was structurally sound — two starters with low walk rates, a HR-suppressing dome, and a flat market line all pointed toward a low-event game. A 10-5 final (15 runs) represents a significant blowup that the underlying profile didn't predict, likely driven by a multi-run inning or early starter exit — exactly the tail risk flagged in the case against. The data was accurate and the reasoning was coherent; the outcome was simply in the bad tail.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient — one high-scoring outlier in a dome game does not invalidate dome suppression factors or low-walk-rate pitcher theses.
